summary: Command execution built from unvalidated user input allows command injection.

findings.title: Command injection via string concatenation in os.popen
findings.evidence: Function ping builds the shell command as 'ping -c 1 ' + host and passes it to os.popen, directly embedding the host parameter into the command string.
findings.impact: If an attacker controls the host value, they can inject additional shell metacharacters and commands (e.g. '8.8.8.8; rm -rf /'), leading to arbitrary command execution with the application's privileges.
findings.recommendation: Avoid building shell commands with string concatenation. Use a safe API such as subprocess.run with a list of arguments and shell=False (e.g. subprocess.run(['ping', '-c', '1', host], check=True, capture_output=True)) and validate host against an allowlist or strict hostname/IP regex before executing.
